titleEn: Sabanto and Verdant Robotics integrate autonomy and precision application systems
titleZh: Sabanto与Verdant Robotics整合自主导航与精准施药系统
summaryZh: 美国公司Sabanto与Verdant Robotics首次整合其自主导航与精准施药系统，实现从田间导航到单株级投入品施用的全自动化作业，无需驾驶舱操作员。该系统已在佛罗里达州Bethel Farms的草皮生产中商业化运行，覆盖超过9900英亩，显著降低人工和投入成本，为农业自动化提供了可落地的集成方案。
translationZh:
美国公司Sabanto Inc.与Verdant Robotics首次整合其系统，使生产者能够实现从导航到单株级投入品施用的田间作业自动化，且无需驾驶舱内操作员。

该整合将Sabanto的自主系统与Verdant Robotics的SharpShooter精准施药系统连接起来。SharpShooter采用“瞄准与施用”技术，可识别单株作物和杂草，仅在需要处施用投入品，现可挂接在配备Sabanto系统的自主拖拉机后方运行。据两家公司称，两套系统现已作为一个集成解决方案协同工作。

拖拉机负责导航并为农具提供动力，而SharpShooter通过CAN总线直接与配备Sabanto系统的拖拉机通信，在主动导航过程中将实时田间数据反馈给Sabanto自主系统。基于这些数据，SharpShooter指示拖拉机根据田间条件调整速度和农具高度。Sabanto表示，这套组合系统可自主执行以往需要驾驶舱内熟练操作员才能完成的决策。

该整合已在多种作物市场的商业运营中运行，包括佛罗里达州阿卡迪亚Bethel Farms的草皮生产。Bethel Farms是一家家族经营的草皮、柑橘和草种苗种植企业，已使用Sabanto自主系统作业超过9900英亩。该公司表示，这套组合系统在苛刻的田间条件下展现了可靠的性能。

**降低投入成本**

“我们的目标始终是让全自主化变得可及且对农业行业中的每个人——农民、农具供应商、零售商、投入品供应商以及拖拉机制造商——立即产生价值，”Sabanto创始人兼首席执行官Craig Rupp表示。“通过将我们的改装自主系统与Verdant的技术配对，我们为生产者和利益相关方提供了一套可在其现有设备上运行的完整解决方案。农民可以24/7运行精准作业，直接应对劳动力短缺问题，同时大幅降低投入成本。”

“投资SharpShooter的种植者旨在减少投入、运行其团队无法始终配备人手的作业，并重新掌控施药窗口，”Verdant Robotics联合创始人兼首席执行官Gabe Sibley强调。“这次整合意味着他们可以在没有驾驶舱操作员的情况下实现这一切——覆盖更多英亩、所需人员更少，且每次作业都保持相同的单株级精准度。”

**改装兼容性**

Sabanto自主系统将高精度GNSS、摄像头硬件安装在改装的拖拉机上。
featuredReason: 该新闻展示了自主导航与精准施药技术的深度集成，为智慧畜牧行业（尤其是草场管理）提供了可借鉴的自动化降本增效方案，对关注劳动力替代和精准投入的从业者具有直接参考价值。
contentFull: Sabanto and Verdant Robotics integrate autonomy and precision application systems 11:00 | Autonomous/semi-autosteering systems | NewsA Sabanto-equipped autonomous tractor pulls a Verdant Robotics SharpShooter unit through a field, running without an operator in the cab. Photos: SabantoU.S. companies Sabanto Inc. and Verdant Robotics have integrated their systems for the first time, enabling producers to automate field work from navigation to plant-level input application without an operator in the cab.The integration connects Sabanto’s Autonomy System with Verdant Robotics’ SharpShooter precision application system. SharpShooter, which uses Aim & Apply technology to identify individual plants and weeds and apply inputs only where needed, now runs behind Sabanto-equipped autonomous tractors. According to the companies, the two systems now function as a single, integrated solution.The tractor handles navigation and powers the implement, while SharpShooter communicates directly with the Sabanto-equipped tractor through the CAN bus, feeding real-time field data to the Sabanto Autonomy System during active navigation. Based on that data, SharpShooter directs the tractor to adjust speed and implement height according to field conditions. Sabanto says the combined system autonomously executes decisions that would previously have required a skilled operator in the cab.The integration is already running in commercial operations across various crop markets, including sod production at Bethel Farms in Arcadia, Florida. Bethel Farms, a family-owned sod, citrus and grass plug grower, has worked over 9,900 acres using Sabanto’s autonomy system. The company says the combined system is demonstrating reliable performance in demanding field conditions. Close-up of the SharpShooter's Aim & Apply system as it targets individual plants and weeds during a field pass.Cutting input costs“Our goal has always been to make full autonomy accessible and immediately valuable for everyone in the ag industry – farmers, implement providers, retailers, input providers, and tractor manufacturers alike”, says Craig Rupp, Founder and CEO of Sabanto. “By pairing our retrofit autonomy system with Verdant’s technology, we’re giving producers and stakeholders a complete solution that works on their existing equipment. Farmers can run precision operations 24/7, addressing labour shortages head-on while dramatically cutting input costs.”“Growers invested in SharpShooter to reduce inputs, run operations their crews can’t always staff, and take back control of their application window,” emphasises Gabe Sibley, Co-Founder and CEO of Verdant Robotics. “This integration means they can do all of that without an operator in the cab – more acres covered, fewer people required, and the same plant-level precision on every pass.” The Sabanto Autonomy System's GNSS and camera hardware mounted on a retrofitted tractor.Retrofit compatibilitySabanto’s autonomy system combines high-precision GNSS, cameras, an advanced vehicle operating system and proprietary path-planning. The integration with SharpShooter is compatible across a wide range of tractor makes and models, in line with Sabanto’s retrofit philosophy of using farmers’ existing equipment.Sabanto is headquartered in Ames, Iowa, with operations in Itasca, Illinois. The company builds aftermarket retrofit kits that convert existing tractors into fully autonomous machines. Verdant Robotics, headquartered in Hayward, California, develops precision application technology for weeding, thinning and input delivery with millimetre-level accuracy. Its SharpShooter system uses what the company describes as physical AI to aim before applying inputs, targeting individual plants and weeds.Join 17,000+ subscribersSubscribe to our newsletter to stay updated about all the need-to-know content in the agricultural sector, two times a week. author: René Groeneveld
contentHtml: U.S. companies Sabanto Inc. and Verdant Robotics have integrated their systems for the first time, enabling producers to automate field work from navigation to plant-level input application without an operator in the cab.
